original_abstract: "Green hydrogen," produced without greenhouse gas emissions, is a cornerstone of the global decarbonization efforts mandated by the Paris Agreement. While proton exchange membrane water electrolyzers (PEMWEs) are poised to play a central role in this transition, their current market presence remains marginal, representing less than one gigawatt of global capacity. To meet climate targets, PEMWE capacity must scale dramatically to hundreds of gigawatts by 2030. This paper provides a comprehensive roadmap for the cell-level advancements required to achieve this massive deployment, incorporating industrial perspectives from three leading hydrogen technology firms. Our analysis covers the critical drivers of growth, including economies of scale, renewable energy pricing, and legislative frameworks, while also addressing technical challenges through accelerated stress testing and component-specific engineering breakthroughs.
humanized_abstract: Green hydrogen, which is free of green gas emissions, is a pivotal element of the transition envisioned by the Paris Agreement. While the share of the proton exchange membrane water electrolyzers (PEMWEs) in the low-carbon hydrogen production market remains minor—below a gigawatt, or about one megawatt per day—climate change economics demands scaling up to hundreds of gigawatts by 2030. In this paper, based on feedback from three manufacturers of hydrogen technologies, we provide cell-level technological roadmap to help identify the keys growth factors: cost and economy of scale; factors related to the cost of renewable energy; legislation. This requires performing accelerated stress tests and technical on-material innovations for each component.

This requires performing accelerated stress tests and technical on-material innovations for each component.
